title: REST API Basics: Resources, HTTP Verbs, and Status Codes
excerpt: A practical beginner-friendly guide to REST APIs: what resources are, how GET/POST/PUT/DELETE work, and which HTTP status codes matter most.

value.title: What a REST API actually is

body.value:
A REST API is a way for systems to communicate over HTTP using clearly defined endpoints.

In practice, it lets one application ask another application for data or request that something be created, updated, or removed.

The most important idea is this: the API exposes resources, and clients interact with those resources using standard HTTP methods.

value.title: Key idea
value.text: Think of a REST API as a contract for working with business resources through HTTP.

body.value:
A resource is usually a business object such as a user, order, invoice, or product.

Endpoints should be named around those resources, not around actions.

  • /api/users
  • /api/orders
  • /api/products

This is more scalable and easier to understand than action-based naming.

• GET - read data
  • POST - create new data
  • PUT - replace existing data
  • PATCH - partially update existing data
  • DELETE - remove data

Even when the back-end is complex, the external API should keep this behavior predictable.

value.caption: Basic REST interaction
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Client] -->|GET /api/orders| B[REST API]
    B --> C[(Database)]
    C --> B
    B -->|200 OK + JSON| A

body.value:
Status codes are part of the API contract. They tell the client what happened.

  • 200 OK - request succeeded
  • 201 Created - new resource created
  • 204 No Content - success with no response body
  • 400 Bad Request - invalid input
  • 401 Unauthorized - authentication required
  • 403 Forbidden - authenticated but not allowed
  • 404 Not Found - resource does not exist
  • 500 Internal Server Error - unexpected server failure

value.title: Practical tip
value.text: Use status codes consistently. Clients should not need to guess whether an operation succeeded.

body.value:
Most REST APIs exchange data as JSON because it is lightweight, readable, and supported almost everywhere.

A typical response might return a collection, a single resource, metadata, or validation errors.

Good APIs use plural nouns, stable URLs, and clear resource hierarchy.

  • /api/customers
  • /api/customers/15/orders

Avoid names like /getOrders or /createUser because HTTP verbs already describe the action.

value.title: Simple controller example in ASP.NET Core

value.caption: Minimal REST-style controller example

body.value:
A good REST API is built around resources, uses HTTP methods consistently, returns meaningful status codes, and keeps naming predictable.

Once these basics are clear, topics like authentication, versioning, pagination, and API security become much easier to understand.

title: Designing RESTful Web APIs — A Practical Guide from Fundamentals to Production
excerpt: Learn how to design RESTful APIs that scale, evolve, and remain maintainable. Covers HTTP fundamentals, resources, verbs, paging, caching, versioning, and security.

value.title: Why API Design Comes Before Implementation

body.value:
One of the biggest mistakes developers make is starting to build an API before designing it. Unlike internal code, APIs become contracts. Once clients depend on them, changing them becomes difficult or even impossible.

A well-designed API is long-lasting, predictable, and easy to evolve. This is why design must come first — not code.

REST APIs are built on top of HTTP, so understanding HTTP is essential. Every request consists of:

  • Verb (what to do)
  • Headers (metadata)
  • Body (data)

Every response contains:

  • Status code
  • Headers
  • Body

value.caption: Basic HTTP request-response flow
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Client -->|Request: GET /customers| Server
Server -->|Response: 200 OK + JSON| Client

body.value:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style based on:

  • Client-server separation
  • Stateless communication
  • Cacheable responses
  • Uniform interface

In practice, REST is about designing intuitive, predictable APIs — not strictly following theory.

In REST, everything is a resource. Resources are represented via URIs:

  • /customers → collection
  • /customers/1 → single resource

Query strings should be used only for optional behavior like filtering or paging.

REST uses HTTP verbs to describe actions:

  • GET → retrieve
  • POST → create
  • PUT → update (full)
  • PATCH → update (partial)
  • DELETE → remove

value.caption: REST operations on resources
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
Collection[/customers/] -->|POST| NewCustomer
Collection -->|GET| ListCustomers
Item[/customers/{id}/] -->|GET| GetCustomer
Item -->|PUT| UpdateCustomer
Item -->|DELETE| DeleteCustomer

body.value:
Idempotency means repeating the same request produces the same result.

  • GET, PUT, DELETE → idempotent
  • POST → NOT idempotent

This is critical for reliability in distributed systems.

Responses should be:

  • Consistent
  • Technology-agnostic
  • Client-friendly

Use JSON conventions like camelCase and avoid leaking database structure.

REST APIs should express relationships via URIs:

  • /customers/1/invoices
  • /projects/22/tickets

This makes APIs intuitive and self-explanatory.

value.caption: Resource relationships
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Customer --> Invoices
Customer --> Projects
Project --> Tickets

body.value:
Never return unbounded collections. Always support paging.

Use query strings like ?page=1&pageSize=25.

Errors should be meaningful and structured.

Use status codes + error body:

Use HTTP caching to reduce load.

ETags allow clients to ask: “Has this changed?”

value.caption: ETag caching flow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
Client->>Server: GET /resource
Server-->>Client: 200 OK + ETag
Client->>Server: GET + If-None-Match
Server-->>Client: 304 Not Modified

body.value:
APIs must evolve without breaking clients.

  • Query string: ?v=1
  • Header: X-Version
  • Content type: application/vnd.api.v1+json

Consistency is more important than the method you choose.

Security must be part of design, not an afterthought.

  • Use HTTPS
  • Implement authentication
  • Apply authorization rules

value.caption: Token authentication flow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
Client->>Server: Login (credentials)
Server-->>Client: JWT Token
Client->>Server: Request + Token
Server-->>Client: Response

body.value:
• Design APIs before implementing
  • Think in resources, not database tables
  • Use HTTP correctly (verbs, status codes)
  • Plan for paging, caching, and versioning
  • Secure your API from day one

Great APIs are not accidental — they are designed.

title: ASP.NET Core — Big Picture Explained
excerpt: Understand what ASP.NET Core is, how it works, and how to build modern web applications using MVC, Blazor, APIs, and more.

body.value:
ASP.NET Core is a modern, cross-platform web application framework built by Microsoft. It allows developers to build both frontend and backend applications using C# and .NET.

It is not just for websites — it is a complete platform for building full web systems including APIs, real-time apps, and modern client-side applications.

value.title: Key idea
value.text: ASP.NET Core is a unified platform for building web applications, APIs, and real-time systems using .NET.

body.value:
A website typically serves static content such as HTML and CSS files.

A web application introduces dynamic behavior — for example fetching data from a database and rendering it on the page.

  • Static site → fixed HTML
  • Web app → dynamic data + logic

value.caption: Static vs dynamic web application
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Browser] --> B[Static HTML]
    A --> C[Web Application]
    C --> D[(Database)]
    D --> C
    C --> A

body.value:
Modern applications separate responsibilities into two layers:

  • Frontend → UI, user interaction
  • Backend → business logic, data access

This separation improves maintainability, scalability, and reusability.

value.caption: Frontend and backend separation
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Browser UI] --> B[Backend API]
    B --> C[(Database)]
    C --> B
    B --> A

value.title: Why separation matters
value.text: A backend API can be reused by web, mobile, and desktop applications.

value.title: Ways to build UI in ASP.NET Core

body.value:
ASP.NET Core provides multiple ways to build frontend applications:

  • MVC → structured pattern (Model-View-Controller)
  • Razor Pages → simpler page-based approach
  • Blazor → modern component-based UI

In server-rendered applications, the server generates HTML and sends it to the browser for each request.

Every interaction (click, form submit) triggers a new request.

value.caption: Server-rendered flow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    Browser->>Server: Request page
    Server->>Database: Fetch data
    Database-->>Server: Data
    Server-->>Browser: HTML response

body.value:
Client-rendered applications shift processing to the browser.

The server sends application logic once, and the browser handles UI updates.

value.caption: Client-rendered SPA flow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    Browser->>Server: Initial request
    Server-->>Browser: App + runtime
    Browser->>API: Fetch data
    API-->>Browser: JSON
    Browser: Render UI

value.title: Key difference
value.text: Server-rendered = HTML per request. Client-rendered = UI handled in browser.

body.value:
A Web API is a backend application that exposes data via HTTP.

  • GET → retrieve data
  • POST → create data
  • PUT → update data

value.caption: REST API interaction
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Client] -->|GET /products| B[API]
    B --> C[(Database)]
    C --> B
    B --> A

value.caption: Example API endpoint

body.value:
ASP.NET Core processes requests using a pipeline of middleware.

Each middleware can inspect or modify the request and response.

value.caption: Request pipeline
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Request] --> B[Routing]
    B --> C[Authorization]
    C --> D[Controller]
    D --> E[Response]

body.value:
Dependency Injection (DI) allows you to register services and request them anywhere in your application.

This makes code modular, testable, and maintainable.

value.title: Why DI matters
value.text: You don’t create dependencies manually — the framework injects them for you.

body.value:
• Multiple frontend approaches (MVC, Razor Pages, Blazor)
  • Powerful backend APIs (REST, gRPC)
  • Built-in dependency injection
  • Flexible middleware pipeline
  • Cross-platform performance

title: ASP.NET Core Web API Fundamentals: From Zero to First API
excerpt: Learn the fundamentals of building APIs with ASP.NET Core, including architecture, middleware, routing, and real-world API design concepts.

body.value:
ASP.NET Core is a cross-platform, high-performance, open-source framework for building modern web applications and APIs. It allows developers to create scalable, cloud-ready applications that run on Windows, Linux, and macOS.

It is commonly used for building REST APIs that communicate with databases, external services, and frontend applications.

At a high level, an ASP.NET Core API processes incoming HTTP requests through a pipeline of components called middleware. Each component can inspect, modify, or stop the request before passing it further.

value.diagram:
graph TD
Client --> Request
Request --> Middleware1
Middleware1 --> Middleware2
Middleware2 --> Controller
Controller --> Response
Response --> Client

body.value: This pipeline is one of the most important concepts in ASP.NET Core. It defines how your application responds to every request.

Middleware are small components executed in sequence. Each middleware can:

  • Handle the request
  • Modify the request or response
  • Stop the pipeline early

The order of middleware is critical. For example, authentication must run before protected endpoints.

value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
Client->>Middleware1: HTTP Request
Middleware1->>Middleware2: Forward
Middleware2->>Controller: Execute action
Controller-->>Middleware2: Result
Middleware2-->>Middleware1: Response
Middleware1-->>Client: Final Response

body.value: The application starts in Program.cs, which configures services and builds the application pipeline.

This file defines:

  • Services (Dependency Injection)
  • Middleware pipeline
  • Application startup behavior

value.title: Understanding the MVC Pattern in APIs

body.value:
ASP.NET Core APIs are typically built using the MVC pattern (Model-View-Controller).

In APIs, the concept of "View" is different — it usually represents JSON output instead of HTML.

value.diagram:
graph TD
Client --> Controller
Controller --> Model
Model --> Controller
Controller --> JSON_Response
JSON_Response --> Client

body.value:
Controller: Handles requests
Model: Represents data
View: JSON response
Routing maps HTTP requests to controller actions. In ASP.NET Core, this is typically done using attribute routing.
This means a GET request to /api/cities will trigger the GetCities method.

APIs rely on HTTP methods to define operations:

  • GET – retrieve data
  • POST – create data
  • PUT – update data
  • DELETE – remove data
Status codes tell the client whether a request succeeded or failed. They are essential for proper API behavior.

value.diagram:
graph TD
Request --> API
API -->|200 OK| Success
API -->|404 Not Found| MissingResource
API -->|400 Bad Request| ClientError
API -->|500 Internal Server Error| ServerError

body.value: Returning incorrect status codes can break client applications and make debugging difficult.
Tools like Postman allow you to send HTTP requests and inspect responses. This is essential when testing APIs beyond simple browser GET requests.

• ASP.NET Core is a powerful framework for building APIs
  • The request pipeline is built using middleware
  • MVC is the standard pattern for API design
  • Routing connects URLs to controller actions
  • Status codes are critical for communication
